stage.name: RAW/WAW hazard: when issue must wait
stage.description: A data-hazard lesson explaining RAW, WAW, x0 exceptions, commit-ignored entries, and why in-order issue blocks younger instructions behind stalled older ones.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

content.title: RAW/WAW hazard: when issue must wait
content.subtitle: Data hazards are not abstract textbook labels; they are issue-stage questions about source operands and destination registers.
content.thesis: ISSUE_STAGE checks whether an instruction can safely read its operands and claim its destination before it is allowed to execute.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: data hazards in issue
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: RAW hazards block issue unless forwarding can provide the operand; WAW hazards block issue when the destination register matches an in-flight scoreboard entry; x0 has no data hazards.

content.speakerNotes: Now we turn hazards into engineering questions. RAW means a source operand may depend on an older destination. WAW means a new destination may collide with an older in-flight destination. In CV32A60X, Issue_read_operands checks these conditions using scoreboard information before it lets the instruction move.

actions.text: Now we turn hazards into engineering questions. RAW means a source operand may depend on an older destination. WAW means a new destination may collide with an older in-flight destination. In CV32A60X, Issue_read_operands checks these conditions using scoreboard information before it lets the instruction move.

scenes.title: RAW: can I read this source yet?

content.title: RAW blocks unless forwarding can satisfy the operand
content.subtitle: A source register that matches an older destination is unsafe unless a valid forwarded value exists.

columns.title: Unsafe by default
columns.body: If a source register of the instruction to issue matches a destination in the scoreboard, issue is blocked.

columns.title: Forwarding escape
columns.body: Forwarding can provide the operand from a returning functional-unit result or a ready scoreboard entry.

content.takeaway: RAW is a wait condition unless the value is already safely available through forwarding.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: data hazards in issue
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: RAW hazards block issue unless forwarding can provide the operand; WAW hazards block issue when the destination register matches an in-flight scoreboard entry; x0 has no data hazards.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: operand forwarding
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Forwarding can take operands from a returning functional-unit result or from a scoreboard entry with a ready non-exception result; forwarding is not possible from CSR instructions.

content.speakerNotes: RAW asks: can this instruction read its source operand yet? If the source register matches an older destination still in the scoreboard, issuing would risk reading stale data. But CVA6 has a forwarding escape. If a non-exception result is already returning, or a scoreboard entry already contains the result, the operand can be replaced with the forwarded value.

actions.text: RAW asks: can this instruction read its source operand yet? If the source register matches an older destination still in the scoreboard, issuing would risk reading stale data. But CVA6 has a forwarding escape. If a non-exception result is already returning, or a scoreboard entry already contains the result, the operand can be replaced with the forwarded value.

scenes.title: WAW: can I claim this destination yet?

content.title: Destination conflicts also block issue
content.subtitle: The issue stage protects write ordering by checking destination registers against in-flight entries.

steps.title: New instruction has rd
steps.body: The candidate instruction wants to write a destination register.

steps.title: Scoreboard has older rd
steps.body: An older in-flight entry already targets the same destination.

steps.title: Issue blocks
steps.body: The new instruction waits so destination ordering remains safe.

steps.title: Commit ignored case
steps.body: Instructions being committed are ignored because they will leave the scoreboard next cycle.

content.takeaway: WAW is about preserving destination ownership among in-flight entries.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: data hazards in issue
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: RAW hazards block issue unless forwarding can provide the operand; WAW hazards block issue when the destination register matches an in-flight scoreboard entry; x0 has no data hazards.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: Scoreboard submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The scoreboard contains a FIFO of issued instructions, has capacity for four entries in this configuration, stores returned results by transaction ID, and exposes oldest completed entries to commit.

content.speakerNotes: WAW asks a different question: can this instruction claim its destination register yet? If an older scoreboard entry has the same destination, the candidate blocks. The source adds a subtle point: instructions being committed are ignored because they will not be in the scoreboard anymore next cycle. That is the sort of detail that keeps a lesson grounded in implementation, not just diagrams.

actions.text: WAW asks a different question: can this instruction claim its destination register yet? If an older scoreboard entry has the same destination, the candidate blocks. The source adds a subtle point: instructions being committed are ignored because they will not be in the scoreboard anymore next cycle. That is the sort of detail that keeps a lesson grounded in implementation, not just diagrams.

content.title: There are no data hazards on x0
content.subtitle: The zero register is architecturally special, so hazard logic treats it specially too.

cards.title: x0 reads as zero
cards.body: Reading x0 does not depend on an older computed value.

cards.title: x0 writes do not create useful state
cards.body: A write to x0 is not a real future operand producer.

cards.title: Hazard exception
cards.body: The source explicitly says there are no data hazards on x0.

cards.title: Teaching rule
cards.body: Mention x0 when explaining RAW/WAW so the rule is not overgeneralized.

content.takeaway: A professional hazard explanation includes architectural special cases.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: data hazards in issue
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: RAW hazards block issue unless forwarding can provide the operand; WAW hazards block issue when the destination register matches an in-flight scoreboard entry; x0 has no data hazards.

content.speakerNotes: The zero register is the friendly exception that makes blanket rules dangerous. The source explicitly says there are no data hazards on x0. That is because x0 reads as zero and does not create meaningful future data through writes. If a course says every register match creates a hazard, it is already slightly wrong. Slightly wrong is where debugging starts its revenge arc.

actions.text: The zero register is the friendly exception that makes blanket rules dangerous. The source explicitly says there are no data hazards on x0. That is because x0 reads as zero and does not create meaningful future data through writes. If a course says every register match creates a hazard, it is already slightly wrong. Slightly wrong is where debugging starts its revenge arc.

scenes.title: One stalled instruction can block the queue

content.title: In-order issue makes old blockers visible
content.subtitle: When an instruction stalls ISSUE_STAGE, next instructions are blocked even if they look independent locally.

items.title: Check the oldest candidate
items.body: You cannot debug the younger instruction first.

items.title: Classify the hazard
items.body: RAW, WAW, structural, flush, or scoreboard capacity.

items.title: Look for forwarding
items.body: A RAW hazard may be bypassed if the value is ready and legal to forward.

items.title: Check x0
items.body: Register zero matches should not be treated like normal data hazards.

items.title: Remember the front-door rule
items.body: A younger instruction waits behind an older stalled candidate.

content.takeaway: In-order issue turns the oldest blocked instruction into the first debugging target.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: Issue_read_operands submodule
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Issue_read_operands tracks hazards, obtains input operands, uses register file values by default, can replace operands with forwarded values, and sends issued instructions to EX_STAGE through a register.

content.speakerNotes: In-order issue gives us a powerful debugging shortcut: look at the oldest blocked candidate first. A younger instruction might look perfectly ready, but it cannot jump ahead. So classify the old blocker: RAW, WAW, structural hazard, flush, or capacity. Then check forwarding and x0. This is how backend stalls become inspectable instead of spooky.

actions.text: In-order issue gives us a powerful debugging shortcut: look at the oldest blocked candidate first. A younger instruction might look perfectly ready, but it cannot jump ahead. So classify the old blocker: RAW, WAW, structural hazard, flush, or capacity. Then check forwarding and x0. This is how backend stalls become inspectable instead of spooky.

questions.question: What is a RAW hazard in this lesson?

options.label: A source register depends on an older scoreboard destination whose value may not be available yet

questions.analysis: RAW means read-after-write: the source operand can depend on an older destination.

questions.question: What is a WAW hazard in this lesson?

options.label: A candidate destination matches an older in-flight destination in the scoreboard
A frontend replay address is selected

questions.analysis: WAW means write-after-write: destination ownership conflicts with an older in-flight entry.

questions.question: Which statements are source-backed for data hazards?

options.label: Forwarding can avoid some RAW stalls
Forwarding is not possible from CSR instructions
There are no data hazards on x0
Instructions being committed are ignored for WAW because they leave the scoreboard next cycle

questions.analysis: All four statements come from the Issue_read_operands hazard description.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: data hazards in issue
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: RAW hazards block issue unless forwarding can provide the operand; WAW hazards block issue when the destination register matches an in-flight scoreboard entry; x0 has no data hazards.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: operand forwarding
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: Forwarding can take operands from a returning functional-unit result or from a scoreboard entry with a ready non-exception result; forwarding is not possible from CSR instructions.

content.speakerNotes: This checkpoint asks whether hazard labels have become concrete. RAW is about source readiness. WAW is about destination ownership. x0 is special. Forwarding can help, but not from CSR. Those four statements will carry us straight into the forwarding lesson.

actions.text: This checkpoint asks whether hazard labels have become concrete. RAW is about source readiness. WAW is about destination ownership. x0 is special. Forwarding can help, but not from CSR. Those four statements will carry us straight into the forwarding lesson.

content.title: RAW and WAW are issue-stage permission checks
content.subtitle: The scoreboard turns register conflicts into explicit wait or forward decisions.
takeaways: RAW asks whether source operands are safely available.
WAW asks whether a destination register can be claimed safely.
x0 and committing entries are important special cases.
content.nextStep: Next: forwarding, where some RAW hazards become legal operand substitutions instead of stalls.
content.closing: Hazard logic is not dramatic. It is just the core asking permission before causing trouble.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: data hazards in issue
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: RAW hazards block issue unless forwarding can provide the operand; WAW hazards block issue when the destination register matches an in-flight scoreboard entry; x0 has no data hazards.

content.speakerNotes: RAW and WAW are now issue-stage permission checks. RAW checks whether a source is safe. WAW checks whether a destination is safe. x0 and committing entries shape the edge cases. Next we focus on forwarding, the feature that lets some RAW hazards become legal operand substitutions instead of stalls.
